speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number three. Before the break, we were talking about the trains and the transport on the trains. Can you tell me what year this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was beginning of December, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next we were loaded, as I said, onto a goods train. The train was shunted onto another line. We were asked to get out. And we-- we were then loaded onto a passenger-- passenger train.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions like on those trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, on the passenger train, we were with German guards. But they saw the element. They-- it wasn't dangerous to them. So they moved. They kept watching us. But they moved to-- to talk to German passengers who were sitting at the rear. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And we went [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from there. I don't know how long the train was running. But we were asked to get out. And the sign, Bialystok, was on the gate. The girls started crying. Because they knew they were further removed from their homes. My home-- my home was not there. So I just cried as much, as they had to have a sort of a credulous performance. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That camp in Bialystok, was not a working camp. It was a transit camp for the human cargo destined for Germany. The sentries were only outside the camp. Inside the camp, you could move-- you could move from one barrack to another. And we had food given to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- sorry. What were the conditions like inside the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had-- there weren't proper mattresses. You know, but we had-- double-- there were double bunks. And people were talking to each other. And everybody was wondering what's in store for them. My-- I was-- some people were only there overnight. I was there several days. And it's that experience that I want to emphasize now, to focus on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just ask you, did you know what was happening in the rest of Europe at that time, what the Germans were doing? Were you worried about where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was worried where I'm going. But on the other hand, getting myself on a slave transport was better than being in a shooting gallery. You had tomorrow to worry about. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And your perception is so immediate, that you don't-- you don't have the maturity to consider the consequences. And you don't have the time. When we came to Bialystok, we were led into a shower-- showers. And we had to hand in our clothing for disinfection. And there were two German women who argued with me because I didn't give them a bra. But I didn't wear one. I didn't have a formed figure at the time. So I told-- told them, I-- I didn't hide it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Next day, I was disappointed. Because, you know, even though I knew I'm not the same as the Poles, I spoke their language. But legitimately, we weren't on equal footing. Even though the Poles came on a racial letter, just after the Jews, they won't-- they weren't actually killing the Poles. Except what we found out later, the-- some of the intelligentsia, when the German entered. But not girls like I was with. Some of these girls were well built and strong. And they were dispatched to Germany before I was. Because they were potential workers in ammunition factories on-- on the farms. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My-- Now I tell you, I was very upset. Because I thought they kept me there because they suspected I was Jewish. But that was only because of the small stature. They didn't quite know what to do with these lighter-built girls, and smaller and younger. The procedure was that every morning you turn up what was called [GERMAN]. That was a counter. And it was actually at this counter they told you, a dispatch. You're going tomorrow, say, to Thuringia. Or you're going this afternoon from this platform, somewhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went there for a few days. And they didn't tell me where I was going. And I was thinking I might be left here. You know? There's no one to advise me. And you-- you dare not ask.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought I was going to Germany, where nobody would know me. And my hope almost died. And I'm ashamed to say it, but I'll tell you. There came a Jew with a nose like this, whose language at home was not Polish, who asked me in a very poor Polish-- he was-- he was flanked by two SS men. He came to seek out the remnants of Jewry. They helped someone escape to that camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said to me, aren't you too young to be here? And I was paralyzed with fear. I didn't answer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For a while, he stood there. And the SS men, ready to pounce. I tell you things a little-- a little bit drawn out at times. And you might think they're trivial.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when I told you I peeled potatoes, it wasn't a solo job. There were some peasants there. And they spoke, amongst themselves, a dialect which as a kid, I picked up. This is very hard to translate. Because when you speak Polish, you'd know that it's a peasant dialect. And I didn't know what to tell this man. And suddenly, like an arrow came through my head. And I answered him in this dialect. No Jew speaks like this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Had I had I a cannon full of bullets, he wouldn't be running as fast. He was ashamed, approaching a-- a peasant offspring, suspecting to be a Jew. He was ashamed in front of these SS men. And he disappeared. But that was my experience at that camp. That's why I go into detail, to tell you this. This was the shameful role of some-- thank God-- rare Jews. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I stayed for a few days. I approached the counter, where I was going. And one day they slipped-- gave me a slip with an obscure address, which said, [INAUDIBLE], Chemnitz. Chemnitz is an industrial town in Saxony. And [INAUDIBLE] is a spinning mill. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And with a group of three girls, three Poles, we arrived in Chemnitz. We had no idea whether we would be going into a camp. We walked in. It was towards the evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train, by passenger train. They kept us separate, but the-- it wasn't-- you know, in separate kind of compartments. I must tell you this because this is a very important part. Before I got to Bialystok, on that train-- I have to reverse this a little bit. You see, I couldn't walk because I had these-- these stitches in my tummy, in my belly. I couldn't tell anyone I escaped from hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the most important part of that journey. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as soon as I could assess-- that was on the way to Bialystok. As soon as I could assess my situation, I found out about the toilets.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody's so tired, that there was little use made of them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went into the toilet. And with a bobby pin, and crying mad, I removed 14 clamps from my belly. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in that sanctuary of the chilly toilet, I just managed to push the lever to get out. And as I came into the compartment, I collapsed. They thought I was emotionally so upset that I was leaving my home, they didn't suspect it. And silence was my best weapon. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So now I have to jump-- Back to Chemnitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to Chemnitz. Yes, because that happened on the train to Bialystok, not to Chemnitz. Not from Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: I already got rid of the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they weren't stitches, you see? At that time, they were using some-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, I would compare it-- it was-- it was torture, having them in. They had some metal component in them. And it reminded you of Kafka's penal-- [? Penalty. ?] You know? Sort of penal place, where you-- were people suffered. And I just couldn't walk anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't want to arouse suspicion. So I had to act and walk like everyone else.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived in Chemnitz, there was no sign of sentry. They converted an old military barracks for a dormitory for these foreign workers. And then appeared a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a German translator, called Brunhilda. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she distributed-- this is a version of it. She distributed a P to us. Because we came from Poland. And we-- we were supposed to wear it in our lapel. And she warned us never to leave the premises without it. This has a continuation, with this tag, of which I shall tell you when we come to it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I hope I don't forget like I forgot the sanctuary of the toilet. Next day, we expected to be collected like the stronger girls in that-- There were some stronger girls in that dormitory. And a lorry came for them to bring them outside the town, to an ammunition factory. It was the three little ones like myself, we got-- we were told how to walk. And without an escort, we went to this spinning mill. When we came there, one of the three girls-- the bravest one-- he knocked-- she knocked at the administrator's office. And he brought us to a machine sort of environment. The machines were screaming there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were sort of separated from each other. And one German woman said, now they're getting them from kindergarten. You know, they were short of workers. The spinning machine was a torture machine for me. It was not-- forget electronics. The machine was going back and forth. And my task was to catch the loose thread and to adjust it with some existing one, with some straight ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: So consequently, your job was you had to walk so many miles a day, probably, to run after the machine, back and forth. That was the motion, all day. The saddest part was that the machine, each time, hit at my abdomen, just where I took out the stitches.

speaker: interviewee
text: It had no mercy. And I had no protective gear. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And after a few days in that factory, I collapsed on the floor. I wouldn't breathe anything to anyone. Because, you know, the Germans were cruel to the Poles too. But they never dragged anyone out of hospital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I couldn't say anything. They put me-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They put me into a foreign barrack. You see, the foreign barracks were meant for sick-- for foreigners who were sick. And the barracks-- these barracks were provisional buildings. And they were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] attached to the main hospital buildings. I want to tell you something about this treatment. It was not a Jewish treatment. This was for foreign workers. Not exclusively Poles, others too. But the ones who do you talk to if you talk a language, you speak a language, Poles. To make it short, I made up a story that I was just out from hospital, and they called me to this work-- for this work on a round up, and here I was. Well, that wasn't the Gestapo. It was a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I want to tell you that the Hippocratical code applicable to patients was only applied to patients who would be fit to join the assembly line very soon. Others were left there to die. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And quite often, at night, I would hear the talk between some clandestine men, asking how many of the poor bastards. And they would carry out bodies like pieces of wood. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But lucky, remember I came out of a liquidation center. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got out from this release after about 10 or 11 days. I returned to my dormitory, that barracks that I told you. Some people made themselves quite comfortable. I was sharing a dig, you might say, with an elderly Russian doctor, a woman, and a Russian teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they became very motherly towards me. You know? It was good. And I spoke a bit of Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I returned on Saturday, which gave me, I thought, another day and a half before I could go to work Saturday afternoon and Sunday. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as I lay fully dressed on my bunk, I hear my name, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and someone calling out. Carolina, you have a visitor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who could it be? The Gestapo? Suddenly, a wrinkled face of a woman became visible. And this woman--

speaker: interviewee
text: Why I told you it has a connection-- this woman told me she is Frau Moebius, the wife of that German supervisor from Lutsk. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] What happened, I must break to tell you. He initiated a search. Not for a Jew, but for a Polish employee. They wouldn't have taken any notice-- the staff at the labor office in Poland would not have taken-- I was registered there as a legitimate Pole-- they wouldn't have taken notice of a Pole searching for another Pole. But this was a German citizen. And everything has to be done by lists. Even lists went off to concentration camps. So this was not a concentration camp. This was a transport to Germany. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] These Moebiuses had a daughter-in-law who was in charge, in her district, of the-- of the [NON-ENGLISH]. That's labor supply office. And she helped her in-laws to trace me. I didn't know whether this woman who came to see me, whether she was foe or friend. I wondered how she found me. When I wondered, as if guessing my question, she told me that her husband initiated a-- that search.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her daughter-in-law, [? Theresa, ?] narrowed it down to this place. So she came to see me. So she asked me, are you well enough to travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wondered what was coming. She wanted to take me to her house. She didn't have a house-- an apartment. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it was [? Theresa-- ?] her daughter-in-law's-- job to establish not just the locality. Because she had the locality, that she qualified to have a foreign lodger. You must know that even foreigners-- [INAUDIBLE] about Jews, there is no talk. But to have a foreign lodger, you had to provide a separate room in case he pollutes. It was racial pollution otherwise. So she had to carry out. It was a small flat. They were poor people. The chairs was old. When you-- you wondered when you sat on them, whether you'd get up. She put up a child's bed belonging to her son, who was in-- in Russia, on the-- at the front. At the house-- at the apartment-- she was telling me-- that they'll be waiting for approval for an inspector. And then I can get on a train and get up-- out at her town, called Waldheim, like [INAUDIBLE]. The town was called Waldheim, so I don't have to spell it for you. I want to tell you now, when I said before, that we both once belonged was under Kaiser Joseph regime. Not in my town. I grew up among German books. And German was spoken in our house. And when I was in Germany, they thought it cute. There's a Polish brat, speaking German. That's Yiddish [YIDDISH], and that's how you make friends. So I got out at that Waldheim station. I ask a lady in the street about the street. I came there. And this woman took me in. I was not there illegally because her daughter-in-law arranged a job for me. And jobs were plenty, but no people to fill them. You see, the Germans were in the army. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She had a knack of not asking questions, not embarrassing me. But she told me she knew I was Jewish. Remember, I confessed to her husband. And she said, this is between you and us. And that daughter-in-law of hers got me a job at 4711. That's the prestigious eau de cologne factory. Only that during the war, they never made eau de cologne. They made-- made a kind of a soap derivative product. And I was packing what they called toothpaste substitute, [GERMAN], into boxes, which was a cushy job-- comparatively cushy job. I lived there with this lady, Frau [? Marta ?] Moebius who was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it-- the treatment-- her treatment of me was a translation of humane-- of humanness. She took off an old blanket from her bed to have it made into a coat for me. She took me along to her friends, as a Pole. She cared for me. She cooked for me. And her daughter-in-law, from the job that I was, wanted to do a favor to her dentist. And she got me into a private household. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The irony, my first impression of the household was that these people were not Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do I know? Well, I must say, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we Jews are also prejudice. We always had a Polish servant. But she never ate at our table. All I was is a servant in that house. But I-- she came up and she said-- she taught me how to set the table. She said what about your place? And I said, with Dr. Winkler? Mrs. Winkler, in their 25-years daughter at the one table. End of tape three.
